title: Multifamily Housing
problem: Milton has the lowest level of multifamily housing among rapid transit communities at just 12.95%. This is a problem because it restricts housing supply, increases costs, and prevents the community from maximizing its transit infrastructure.
solution: Milton needs the 'Density and Mix of Uses' principle because it advocates for zoning that allows compact, diverse housing types right next to transit stations, increasing supply and creating walkable, vibrant neighborhoods.
alt: Drawn image of a TOD neighborhood with mixed-use buildings highlighted

title: Rent Burden
problem: Rockport has the highest rent burden, with 36% of its population paying at least 50% of their income on rent. This is a problem because it causes severe financial instability and risks displacing long-time or vulnerable residents.
solution: Rockport needs the 'Equitable Development' principle because it prioritizes creating and preserving affordable housing near transit. It ensures people of all income levels can live near stations, reducing both their housing and transportation costs.
alt: Drawn image of a TOD neighborhood with affordable housing and public transportation highlighted

title: Resistance to Climate Change
problem: Chelsea has a 79% Resistance to Climate Change. While high, urban heat islands are a problem because dense, paved areas without shade amplify rising climate temperatures, causing public health risks.
solution: Chelsea needs 'A Great Public Realm' because it emphasizes sustainable, climate-resilient infrastructure. It encourages planting street trees, adding green spaces, and using heat-reducing materials to cool the path to transit.
alt: Drawn image of a TOD neighborhood with a bike lane, crosswalk, and wheelchair-accessible ramps highlighted

title: Disability Accessibility
problem: In Fall River, 21% of the population experiences high levels of disability. This is a problem because poor streetscapes and physical barriers can make it unsafe or impossible for them to access public transit.
solution: Fall River needs 'A Great Public Realm' because it requires universal design. It focuses on building ADA-compliant sidewalks, safe crosswalks, and accessible ramps so everyone has equal access to transit stations.
alt: Drawn image of a TOD neighborhood with a bike lane, crosswalk, and wheelchair-accessible ramps highlighted

title: Parking Lot Area Nearby
problem: Communities like Worcester, Kingston, and Grafton lack parking near their commuter rail stations. This is a problem because people in these spread-out Zone 8 towns cannot reasonably access the train if they cannot park near it.
solution: These communities need 'A TOD Approach to Parking' because it focuses on 'right-sizing' parking. It encourages building efficient, shared parking structures or boosting multi-modal access (like shuttle buses and bike paths) so commuters can easily reach the station.
alt: Drawn image of a TOD neighborhood with a parking lot highlighted
